question_text: When two or more position indications are observed in close proximity, or are observed to be making similar movements at the same time, or when doubt exists as to the identity of a position indication for any other reason, changes of _______ should be prescribed or repeated as many times as necessary, or additional methods of identification should be employed, until all risk of error in identification is eliminated: from question bank
explanation: Changes of heading help confirm identity.

question_text: While performing duty as a radar controller you accept a radar handover for an aircraft. Which SSR method of radar identification is most appropriate in this case? from question bank

options.A: Recognition of an assigned discrete code, the setting of which has been verified.
options.B: Recognition of the aircraft identification in a radar label.
options.C: Observation of compliance with an instruction to set a specific code.

explanation: Transfer of radar identification is the method.

question_text: An aircraft is having problem with selection of a given code by ATC. Which of the following can be most appropriate SSR method of identification in this case? from question bank

options.A: Recognition of an assigned discrete code, the setting of which has been verified.
options.B: Observation of compliance with an instruction to squawk IDENT.
options.C: Observation of compliance with an instruction to set a specific code.

explanation: If code selection fails, use IDENT.

question_text: When SSR and PSR both are collocated; PSR method of identification is more reliable. from question bank
explanation: SSR identification is more reliable.

question_text: When working on a PSR an aircraft reports over head Nawabshah and informs you proceeding from OPSK to OPKC. The radar blip you observe is consistent with the position report and movement of the aircraft. Identification can be done without giving a turn of 30 degree or more. from question bank
explanation: If position report matches blip, identification is established.

question_text: When working on a PSR; an aircraft reports 35 southwest of Lahore proceeding to Lahore. The radar blip you observe is consistent with the position report of the aircraft. Identification can be done without executing any other method of identification. from question bank
explanation: Position report consistency is enough.

question_text: You can identify this aircraft within 01NM from the runway in use after getting air-born by executing one of the SSR methods of identification. from question bank
explanation: Within 1 NM from runway, identification is not required; radar contact can be established by departure method.

question_text: An aircraft reports orbiting overhead Sheikhupura and with no transponder on board. As an approach controller you are observing a only one radar blip consistent with maneuvers reported by the aircraft. You can identify this aircraft without executing any other method of identification. from question bank
explanation: If the blip is consistent with reported orbiting, identification is established.

question_text: Which of the following action needs to be ensured before transferring radar identity by a radar controller to another radar controller. from question bank

options.A: Aircraft is exiting the radar coverage.
options.B: Aircraft is within the radar coverage of the accepting controller.
options.C: Accepting unit is able to handle the traffic being transferred.
options.D: Accepting controller is experienced enough to handle the traffic being transferred.

explanation: The accepting controller must have radar coverage and ability; experience is not a formal requirement.

question_text: Which of the following is an incorrect method of transfer of radar identification? from question bank

options.A: Designation of the radar position indication over the shoulder by voice.
options.B: Designation of radar position indication by automated means.
options.C: Notification of the discrete code of the aircraft.
options.D: Direct designation by pointing a finger.

explanation: Pointing a finger is not a standard method; voice or automated means are used.

question_text: In which of the following circumstances, an aircraft provided with the radar service should be informed of its position. from question bank

options.A: Upon identification based on pilot report of aircraft position.
options.B: Upon identification within 01 NM of the runway after getting air-born.

explanation: The answer key says 'None of the above' – position information is not required in those specific cases.

question_text: An aircraft provided with radar service should be informed of its position: from question bank

options.A: When pilot is instructed to resume own navigation after radar vectoring.
options.B: When pilot estimate differ significantly from the radar controller.
options.D: All of the above are correct.

explanation: All three situations require position information.

question_text: Aircraft shall not be vectored closer than a distance equivalent to one half of the prescribed separation minimum, from limits of the airspace for which the radar controller is responsible unless: from question bank

options.A: Transfer of radar control is to be effected.
options.B: Accepting radar controller is well known.
options.C: Local arrangements ensure separation with radar controlled aircraft operating in the adjacent areas.
options.D: Both a) and c) are correct.

explanation: Vectoring can be closer if transfer is imminent or local arrangements exist.

question_text: Position information shall not be passed to an aircraft as a distance and direction from the centre line of an ATS route. from question bank
explanation: Position information may be passed as distance and direction from a route centre line.

question_text: When so informed the pilots may omit position reports. Pilot shall resume position reporting when: from question bank

options.C: Informed of loss of radar identification.
options.D: All of the above are correct.

explanation: Resume reporting in all those cases.

question_text: Which of the following is an advantage of vectoring the aircraft along the ATS routes? from question bank

options.A: Pilot can monitor the aircraft position with reference to pilot interpreted Nav aids.
options.B: It will minimize the amount of radar navigation assistance required.
options.C: It will alleviate the consequences resulting from radar failure.

explanation: All are advantages.

question_text: While vectoring, an aircraft may not be informed of the purpose of the vector. from question bank
explanation: Purpose may be omitted if not necessary.

question_text: Controlled flights may be vectored into uncontrolled airspace: from question bank
explanation: Vectors into uncontrolled airspace are not permitted for those reasons.

question_text: Which of the following should be done prior to the issuing of manoeuvring instructions an aircraft which has reported unreliable directional instruments: from question bank

options.A: Request pilot to make all turns at an agreed rate.
options.B: Inform pilot of the weather at the alternate aerodrome.
options.C: Request pilot to carry out instruction immediately upon receipt.
options.D: Both a) and c) are correct.

explanation: Agree on turn rate and instruct immediate action.

question_text: The Short Term Conflict Alert (STCA) can be activated/inhibited by the controller from SDD position. from question bank
explanation: STCA is usually system-automated; controller may inhibit via command, but the statement as given may be false.

question_text: Radar controller retains the responsibility of terrain clearance for IFR flights. from question bank
explanation: For IFR flights, controller ensures terrain clearance when vectoring.

question_text: Whenever possible, minimum vectoring altitude should be sufficiently high in order to: from question bank

options.A: Give sufficient room to the pilot to be visual with airfield.
options.B: Minimize the activation of Ground Proximity Warning System (GPWS).
options.C: To minimize the work load of radar controller.
options.D: To minimize the track miles of the aircraft being vectored.

explanation: Sufficient altitude reduces GPWS alerts.

question_text: An aircraft provided with ATS surveillance service should be informed of its position upon identification, except when the identification is established by transfer of identification. from question bank
explanation: Upon transfer, position is already known.

question_text: The pilot of an aircraft requesting navigation assistance from an air traffic control unit providing radar service shall state reason. from question bank
explanation: Reason for assistance request must be given.

question_text: Information that an aircraft is going to penetrate an area of adverse weather shall be issued by the radar controller in sufficient time to permit the pilot to decide on appropriate course of action. from question bank
explanation: Timely weather information is required.

question_text: An aircraft's weather radar will normally provide better detection and definition of adverse weather than ATC radar. from question bank
explanation: Airborne weather radar is more sensitive.

question_text: In vectoring an aircraft for circumnavigating an area of adverse weather, the radar controller should ascertain that: from question bank

options.A: The aircraft can be returned to its departure point.
options.B: The aircraft can be returned to its intended flight path within the available radar coverage.
options.C: The pilot is informed about the circumstances in case the radar controller is unable to ensure b).
options.D: Both b) and c) are correct.

explanation: Ensure return to flight path or inform if not possible.

question_text: Areas of adverse weather are always visible on a ATC radar display. from question bank
explanation: Not all weather is visible on ATC radar.

question_text: Which of the following is not a function of ATC radar? from question bank
explanation: Detecting weather is not a primary function of ATC radar (though possible).

question_text: Which of the following is a function of ATC radar? from question bank

options.A: Provide radar vectoring to aircraft to resolve conflicts.
options.B: Provide separation between RCF aircraft and other aircraft.
options.C: Provide vectoring around areas of adverse weather.

explanation: All are functions.

question_text: Which of the following is not a function of ATC radar? from question bank

options.A: An integral part in the establishment of RVSM airspace.
options.B: Provide vectoring for efficient approach sequence.
options.C: Provide vectoring for expeditious departure flow.
options.D: Provide vectoring to departing traffic for expeditious climb to cruising level.

explanation: Radar is not an integral part of RVSM establishment.

question_text: Radar separation shall only be provided: from question bank

options.B: When procedural separations cannot resolve traffic conflict
options.D: To all the aircraft whose blips are visible on radar display.

explanation: Radar separation only for identified aircraft.

question_text: An identified aircraft may not be provided with radar separation if in the opinion of radar controller the identification cannot be maintained. from question bank
explanation: If identification cannot be maintained, radar separation is not applied.

question_text: A synthetic track is a track generated by the system upon controller request. The information displayed on synthetic track is based upon: from question bank
explanation: Synthetic track uses flight plan data.

question_text: Radar separation based on the use of PSR blips and SSR responses shall be applied so that the distance between the ______ is never less than a prescribed minimum. from question bank

options.A: Center of the PSR blip and the nearest edge of the SSR response.
options.B: Nearest edge of the PSR blip and the center of the SSR response
options.C: Center of the PSR blip and the center of the SSR response

explanation: Distance between center of PSR blip and nearest edge of SSR response.

question_text: Radar separation based on the use of SSR responses shall be applied so that the distance between the _____ of the SSR responses is never less than a prescribed minimum. from question bank

options.C: Both a) and b) are correct.

explanation: Distance between closest edges.

question_text: Radar separation based on the use of SSR responses shall be applied so that the distance between the centers of the SSR responses is never less than a prescribed minimum if: from question bank

options.A: Performance of the radar is reliable.
options.C: The SSR responses from the wide body aircraft.

explanation: Center-to-center separation requires authorization.

question_text: Radar separation based on the use PSR blips shall be applied so that the distance between the centers of the PSR blips is never less than a prescribed minimum. Attention may be drawn to the fact that under certain circumstances the edges of PSR blips may touch each other. from question bank
explanation: For PSR, separation is between edges, not centers.

question_text: Radar separation may be applied between an aircraft taking off and a preceding departing aircraft or other radar controlled traffic provided that: from question bank

options.A: Aircraft taking off has a required db rating.
options.B: There is reasonable assurance that the departing air craft will be identified with 01 NM from the end of the runway.
options.C: There is reasonable assurance that radar failure will not occur.
options.D: The radar controller takes control of the departing aircraft before departure.

explanation: Must be identified within 1 NM.

question_text: Radar Separation may be applied between aircraft holding over the same holding point. from question bank
explanation: Aircraft holding over same point cannot be separated by radar.

question_text: Radar separation may be applied between holding aircraft and other flights subject to: from question bank

options.A: Consent from the pilot in command of the aircraft involved.
options.B: Requirements and approval from the appropriate ATS authorities.

explanation: Approval from ATS authorities required.

question_text: Unless otherwise prescribed, the horizontal separation minimum based on radar and/or ADS-B and/or MLAT systems shall be Doc4444/8.7.3.1: from question bank
explanation: Standard radar separation is 5 NM.

question_text: Subject to approval from appropriate ATS authorities and other specified conditions, minimum radar separation between succeeding aircraft established on the same final approach track is: from question bank
explanation: On final approach, 2.5 NM may be used.

question_text: Wake turbulence radar separation minima for Medium aircraft behind Heavy is 5 NM when both aircraft are using parallel runways separated by more the 760 meters. from question bank
explanation: For parallel runways separated by more than 760 m, normal separation (not wake) applies.

question_text: An aircraft encountering a state of emergency which has previously been directed by ATC to operate the transponder on a specific code, shall be advised to set code A 7700. from question bank
explanation: Pilot should set 7700 without being advised.

question_text: Which of the following is the identification method using primary radar? from question bank

options.C: Position report from the aircraft method.

explanation: All are PSR identification methods.

question_text: When an identified IFR flight operating outside controlled airspace is observed to be on a conflicting path with an other aircraft, the pilot should be: from question bank

options.A: Informed as to the need for collision avoidance action to be initiated.
options.B: Suggested a course of avoiding action if situation warrants.
options.C: Suggested a course of avoiding action on request.
options.D: All of the above are correct.

explanation: All are appropriate.

question_text: Which of the following information should be passed to a identified controlled flight regarding an unknown traffic on a conflicting path? from question bank

options.A: Relative bearing and distance of the conflicting traffic.
options.B: Direction in which unknown conflicting traffic seems to be moving.
options.D: All of the above are correct.

explanation: All may be passed.

question_text: When an identified controlled flight is observed to be on a conflicting path with an unknown aircraft deemed to constitute a collision hazard, the pilot of the controlled flight shall whenever practicable: from question bank

options.A: Be informed of the unknown aircraft.
options.B: Be suggested with course of avoiding action if situation warrants.
options.C: Be suggested with course of avoiding action on request.
options.D: All of the above are correct.

explanation: All actions.

question_text: If two way communication is lost with an aircraft, the radar controller should: from question bank

options.B: Inform other aircraft in the vicinity.
options.C: Plot the position the position of the aircraft on the radar.
options.D: Determine whether the receiver of the aircraft is working.

explanation: Must determine if receiver is working.

question_text: Radar separation can be applied using the synthetic target. from question bank
explanation: Synthetic target is not used for radar separation.

question_text: When an identified IFR flight operating outside controlled airspace is observed to be on a conflicting path with an other unidentified aircraft then an immediate avoiding vector shall be initiated by the radar controller to prevent collision: from question bank
explanation: Vectoring may not be immediate; traffic information may suffice.

question_text: If two way communication is lost with an aircraft the radar controller should determine whether the receiver of the aircraft is working by instructing the aircraft to acknowledge by: from question bank

options.D: All of the above are correct.

explanation: All methods can be used.

question_text: When it has been determined that the receiver of the RCF aircraft is working, the control of the aircraft may be continued if: from question bank

options.A: Aircraft can acknowledge instructions by making specified manoeuvres.
options.B: Aircraft is equipped with S/A transponder.
options.C: Aircraft is in the position to continue with the flight plan.
options.D: Aircraft is in the controlled airspace and can be separated with other flights.

explanation: If equipped with S/A transponder (Mode S), control can continue.

question_text: If a controlled aircraft experiences complete RCF in the radar covered airspace, the radar separation may continue to be used between RCF aircraft and other aircraft. from question bank
explanation: Radar separation can continue if identification is maintained.

question_text: If the aircraft experiencing complete RCF is unidentified, the radar separation may continue to be used provided: from question bank

options.A: The performance of the radar is satisfactory for the purpose.
options.B: Radar separation between identified aircraft and all the unidentified aircraft along the expected route of RCF aircraft is ensured.
options.C: Radar separation between identified aircraft can be ensured.

explanation: Separation must be ensured between identified and all unidentified on that route.

question_text: What should be the action if an aircraft experiences transponder failure after departure and the carriage of transponder is mandatory is the airspace. from question bank

options.A: Aircraft may be returned to the departure aerodrome.
options.B: Aircraft may be permitted to continue to the destination preferably.
options.C: Aircraft may asked to land at the nearest suitable aerodrome if acceptable to the operator.

explanation: All options are possible.

question_text: The final vector shall enable the aircraft to be established in level flight on the final approach track prior to intercepting the glide path. The final vector should provide an intercept angle with the final approach path of: from question bank
explanation: Intercept angle should be 45 degrees or less.

question_text: Radar controller may initiate radar vectoring for visual approach to an IFR flight provided: from question bank

options.A: The aircraft is number one in the approach sequence.
options.B: VMC prevail and reasonable assurance exists that visual approach and landing can be made.
options.C: Ceiling is above the minimum vectoring altitude.
options.D: Both b) and c) are correct.

explanation: VMC and ceiling above MVA are required.

question_text: Clearance for visual approach shall be issued only after the pilot has reported: from question bank
explanation: Runway or preceding traffic in sight.

question_text: An aircraft making SRA should be directed to execute missed approach on the instruction of the aerodrome controller. from question bank
explanation: SRA missed approach is directed by tower.

question_text: The tolerance value used to determine that Mode C-derived level information displayed to the controller for an RVSM compliant aircraft is accurate shall be: from question bank
explanation: In RVSM, accuracy tolerance is ±200 ft.

question_text: The tolerance which shall be used to determine that ADS level information displayed to the controller in RVSM airspace is accurate shall be: from question bank
explanation: ADS level accuracy in RVSM is ±200 ft.

question_text: Codes shall be reserved internationally for use by pilots encountering a state of emergency, radio communication failure or unlawful interference, respectively. from question bank
explanation: 7700=emergency, 7600=radio failure, 7500=hijack.

question_text: The RPM of Thomson CSF Primary Radar is: from question bank
explanation: Thomson CSF primary radar rotates at 10 RPM.

question_text: The speed for a given aircraft weight and runway conditions to which an aircraft can be accelerated and then brought to a stop in the remaining runway is known as: from question bank
explanation: V1 is the decision speed for take-off.

question_text: Which of the following is the disadvantage of primary radar? from question bank
explanation: All are disadvantages.

question_text: Where PSR and SSR are required to be used in combination, SSR alone may be used in the event of PSR failure to provide separation between ______, provided the accuracy of the SSR position indications has been verified by monitor equipment or other means. from question bank
explanation: SSR alone can separate identified transponder-equipped aircraft.

question_text: An aircraft flying in advisory airspace shall be provided with: from question bank
explanation: Advisory airspace gets radar advisory service.

question_text: An aircraft may be authorized by ATC to operate with an unserviceable transponder in airspace where carriage of serviceable transponder is mandatory provided that: from question bank

options.A: Pilot puts up request on R/T.
options.C: Operator puts up request recorded line.

explanation: Operator must put up request on recorded line.

question_text: Misaligned radar scope may be used for radar monitoring service provided due allowance is given for the extent of misalignment. from question bank
explanation: If allowance is given, misalignment is acceptable.

question_text: Radar scope alignment accuracy checks can be made by: from question bank

options.A: Comparison of observed aircraft tracks with known VOR radial, ILS localizer course.
options.B: Comparison of target track with controller-assigned heading.
options.C: Comparison of aircraft position reports with known geographic locations.
options.D: All of the above are correct.

explanation: All methods are valid.

question_text: Which of the following is a correct method of passing weather cells information to an aircraft? from question bank

options.A: Range and bearing of the center of the observed cell echo from the airport or other well known location.
options.B: Orientation and length of major and minor axis.
options.C: Apparent intensity, direction and speed of movement of the cell.

explanation: All are correct.

question_text: For SAR purposes, which of the following is required to be preserved by the radar controller when a radar-tracked aircraft believed to be experiencing an emergency is lost from radar contact? from question bank

options.C: The time at which radar contact ended.

explanation: All must be preserved.

question_text: The civil SSR identification mode (Mode-A) is the same as the military IFF/SIF mode (Mode-3). IFF stands for? from question bank
explanation: IFF stands for Identification of Friend or Foe.

question_text: If the colour of the label shows the call sign in pink colour it means: from question bank

options.A: error in the Squawk of aircraft with the squawk in FPL
options.B: error in FL of aircraft with FL in FPL

explanation: Pink indicates squawk mismatch.

question_text: Whenever an apparent discrepancy is observed between the SSR code given to an aircraft and the displayed result, the controller shall request the pilot to confirm the code and reset his transponder using the phrase: from question bank
explanation: Phrase: Reset squawk (mode) (code).

question_text: Whenever an apparent discrepancy is observed between the SSR code given to an aircraft and the displayed result, the controller shall request the pilot to confirm the code and reset his transponder. If discrepancy still persists the pilot may be asked to: from question bank

options.C: Squawk 0000 followed by instruction to reselect the assigned code.

explanation: Pilot may be asked to squawk 0000 and reselect.

question_text: You must have read the phraseologies SQUAWK LOW or SQUAWK NORMAL. These phraseologies are related with: from question bank
explanation: They relate to the IDENT feature? Actually SQUAWK LOW/NORMAL are for transponder sensitivity? The answer key says C.

question_text: Which of the following is the correct phraseology to request a pilot for temporary suspension of transponder operation? from question bank
explanation: SQUAWK STANDBY is used.

question_text: Which of the following is the correct phraseology to request a pilot to squawk emergency mode? from question bank

options.C: SQUAWK EMEGENCY MODE A [ CODE SEVEN-SEVEN-ZERO-ZERO]

explanation: Correct phrase is SQUAWK MAYDAY [CODE SEVEN-SEVEN-ZERO-ZERO].

question_text: Which of the following is the correct phraseology to request a pilot for termination of transponder operation? from question bank
explanation: STOP SQUAWK is correct.

question_text: Vertical separation using Mode C is not to be applied against aircraft with transponder Mode A0000. from question bank
explanation: Mode A0000 indicates no altitude reporting.

question_text: An aircraft cleared to a level will be considered to have commenced its manoeuvre and vacated the previously occupied level when the SSR Mode-C derived level information indicates a change of: from question bank

options.A: More than 300 feet in the anticipated direction.
options.B: More than 200 feet in the anticipated direction.
options.C: More than 100 feet in the anticipated direction.

explanation: Vacated when change >300 ft.

question_text: Invalid or invalidated Mode-C read-outs shall not be used to determine an aircraft's altitude but may be used to observe whether an aircraft is climbing, descending or is in level flight. from question bank
explanation: Invalid data can still show trends.

question_text: An aircraft may be unable to accept or continue to comply with ATC speed restrictions in turbulent conditions. from question bank
explanation: Turbulence may prevent speed compliance.

question_text: Formation flight should not normally be subjected to speed control. from question bank
explanation: Formation flights are not speed controlled.

question_text: Radio communications with the aircraft shall be retained by the transferring radar controller until accepting radar controller has agreed to assume responsibility for providing radar services to the aircraft. from question bank
explanation: Transfer only after acceptance.

question_text: If an anticipation radar hand-over cannot be accomplished for any reason, the transferring radar controller shall: from question bank

options.A: Establish non-radar separation between the subject aircraft.
options.B: Terminate radar control service with the subject aircraft.
options.C: Request the aircraft to inform the accepting unit of the separation between them.
options.D: Both a) and b) are correct.

explanation: Establish non-radar separation or terminate service.

question_text: Radar controllers have the responsibility for the terrain clearance to aircraft in receipt of a radar information service when not subject to radar vectors. from question bank
explanation: If not vectoring, pilot is responsible for terrain.

question_text: Radar controllers have the responsibility for the terrain clearance to operating Special VFR within controlled airspace provided with radar vectors. from question bank
explanation: When vectoring, controller ensures terrain clearance even for Special VFR.

question_text: The extent of radar service which an Approach Control unit may provide will depend upon the: from question bank

options.B: Operational procedures approved by the Appropriate ATS authority.
options.C: Types of navigational aids available at the aerodrome.

explanation: All factors influence service extent.

question_text: Which of the following can be the function of an approach radar controller? from question bank
explanation: All are approach radar functions.

question_text: The approach radar controller when assessing distance must take into account: from question bank

options.A: Distance indicated by range rings is measured from the radar antenna head.
options.B: Distance indicated by range marks on extended center (video) is measured from touchdown.

explanation: All factors must be considered.

question_text: Critical military aircraft shall not normally be subjected to speed control. from question bank
explanation: Critical military flights are exempt.

question_text: Aircraft carrying out surveillance radar approaches shall be informed of the: from question bank
explanation: All must be informed.

question_text: Vectoring should be arranged so that an aircraft closes the final approach track at not less than miles from touchdown. from question bank
explanation: Not less than 5 NM from touchdown.

question_text: If glide path is inoperative; the pilot cleared for a 'localizer only' procedure may request advisory heights appropriate to the surveillance radar approach for the runway. from question bank
explanation: Advisory heights may be provided.

question_text: A multi-engine turbojet or a multi-engine propeller driven aircraft between 30 to 50 NM from the airfield shall not be instructed to reduce speed below: from question bank
explanation: Minimum speed is 250 kts in that range.

question_text: A multi-engine turbojet or a multi-engine propeller driven aircraft between 20 to 30 NM from the airfield and above F 100, shall not be instructed to reduce speed below: from question bank
explanation: Above F100, minimum 250 kts.

question_text: A multi-engine turbojet or a multi-engine propeller driven aircraft between 20 to 30 NM from the airfield and below F 100 shall not be instructed to reduce speed below: from question bank
explanation: Below F100, minimum 200 kts.

question_text: A multi-engine turbojet aircraft less than 20 NM from the airfield shall not be instructed to reduce speed below: from question bank
explanation: Less than 20 NM, minimum 180 kts.

question_text: Landing clearance or alternative instructions must be passed to an aircraft making a Surveillance radar approach before it reaches: from question bank
explanation: Before reaching 2 NM.

question_text: Aircraft making a Surveillance radar approach shall be reminded, when on final approach, to check that wheels are down and locked. from question bank
explanation: Standard reminder.

question_text: When carrying out Surveillance radar approach, the radar controller shall not be made responsible for any duties other than those directly connected with the SRA. from question bank
explanation: Controller focuses on SRA only.

question_text: Which of the following shall apply in case the termination range is 2 NM for a surveillance radar approach: from question bank

options.A: Aircraft shall be informed of the termination range.
options.B: Advisory heights and ranges shall be passed at each NM.
options.C: The pilot shall be instructed to check decision height at a range of 3 NM from touch down.

explanation: All actions apply.

question_text: An aircraft on Surveillance radar approach shall be instructed to carry out a missed approach when: from question bank

options.B: When no landing clearance is received till 2 NM from touchdown
options.C: When the aircraft appears to be dangerously positioned on final approach.

explanation: All are valid reasons.

question_text: A specified altitude or height in a 3D instrument approach operation at which a missed approach must be initiated if the required visual reference to continue the approach has not been established: from question bank
explanation: Definition of Decision Altitude/Height (DA/H).

question_text: A defined rectangular area on the ground or water under the control of appropriate authority selected or prepared as a suitable area over which an aeroplane may make a portion of its initial to a specified height. from question bank
explanation: Definition of clearway.

question_text: Over high terrain and mountainous areas the minimum obstacle clearance is: from question bank
explanation: In mountainous areas, minimum obstacle clearance is 2000 ft.

question_text: Radio telephony logs shall be retained at the location for at least: from question bank
explanation: RTF logs retained for 120 days.

question_text: Flight progress strips shall be retained at the location for at least: from question bank
explanation: Flight progress strips retained for 120 days.

question_text: Messages in fixed service that need special handling should be assigned priority indicator. from question bank
explanation: Special handling messages use priority DD.

question_text: Runway of Gawadar Airport is a: from question bank
explanation: Gawadar runway is non-precision approach.

question_text: No aircraft shall be operated within Karachi and Lahore CTA, CTR and Cherat CTR unless equipped with a serviceable transponder including Mode C. from question bank
explanation: Transponder including Mode C is required above FL250, not all areas.

question_text: No aircraft shall be operated within Karachi and Lahore CTA, CTR and Cherat CTR unless equipped with a serviceable transponder. from question bank
explanation: Transponder required in those areas.

question_text: What is the next flight level available after F410 on tracks 000 to 179 degrees? from question bank
explanation: Above F410, next levels are in 2000 ft increments; for eastbound, F450.

question_text: What is the next RVSM Flight level available on tracks between 180 to 359 degrees after F400? from question bank
explanation: RVSM ends at F410; after F400, the next is F410, but that is still RVSM; after F410, the next non-RVSM level on westbound tracks is F450? Actually the answer key says 'None of the above'.

question_text: A runway served by visual aids and non-visual aid(s) intended for landing operations following an instrument approach operation type A and a visibility not less than 1 000 m., is categorized as: from question bank
explanation: Non-precision approach runway type A with visibility ≥1000m.

question_text: A runway served by visual aids and non-visual aid(s) intended for landing operations following an instrument approach operation type B with a decision height (DH) lower than 60 m (200 ft) but not lower than 30 m (100 ft) and a runway visual range not less than 300, is categorized as: from question bank

options.D: Precision approach runway CAT III A
options.E: Precision approach runway CAT III B

explanation: CAT II has DH 30-60 m and RVR ≥300 m.

question_text: A runway served by visual aids and non-visual aid(s) intended for landing operations following an instrument approach operation type B with a decision height (DH) not lower than 60 m (200 ft) and either a visibility not less than 800 m or a runway visual range not less than 550 m, is categorised as: from question bank

options.D: Precision approach runway CAT III A
options.E: Precision approach runway CAT III B

explanation: CAT I has DH ≥60 m and visibility ≥800 m or RVR ≥550 m.

question_text: A runway served by visual aids and non-visual aid(s) intended for landing operations following an instrument approach operation type B to and along the surface of the runway and intended for operations with a decision height (DH) lower than 30 m (100 ft), or no decision height and a runway visual range not less than 175 m, is categorized as: from question bank

options.C: Precision approach runway CAT III A
options.D: Precision approach runway CAT III B
options.E: Precision approach runway CAT III C

explanation: CAT III A has DH <30 m or no DH, RVR ≥175 m.

question_text: A runway served by visual aids and non-visual aid(s) intended for landing operations following an instrument approach operation type B to and along the surface of the runway and intended for operations with a decision height (DH) lower than 15 m (50 ft), or no decision height and a runway visual range less than 175 m but not less than 50 m, is categorized as: from question bank

options.A: Precision approach runway. CAT I II
options.B: Precision approach runway CAT III A
options.C: Precision approach runway CAT III B
options.D: Precision approach runway CAT III C

explanation: CAT III B has DH <15 m or no DH, RVR between 50 m and 175 m.

question_text: The length of an arc of a great circle which subtends an angle of one degree at the center of the earth is called: from question bank
explanation: One nautical mile is defined as one minute of latitude, which is 1/60 of a degree.

question_text: A runway intended for the operation of aircraft using visual approach procedures or an instrument approach procedure to a point beyond which the approach may continue in visual meteorological conditions. is known as: from question bank
explanation: Non-instrument runway is for visual approaches or IAP to VMC point.

question_text: A number expressing the bearing strength of a pavement for unrestricted operations is known as: from question bank
explanation: PCN is Pavement Classification Number.

question_text: SSR code used by the aircraft in emergency: from question bank
explanation: 7700 is emergency.

question_text: Decision altitude / height is a specified altitude or height (A/H) in the precision approach at which a missed approach must be initiated if the required visual reference to continue the approach has not been established. What is the reference datum for decision height? from question bank
explanation: Decision Height is referenced to the threshold elevation.

question_text: Minimum Sector Altitude is determined for: from question bank
explanation: MSA is for IFR flights.

question_text: Information issued by a meteorological watch office concerning the occurrence or expected occurrence of specified en-route weather phenomena which may affect the safety of aircraft operations are known as: from question bank
explanation: Definition of SIGMET.

question_text: The projection on the earth's surface of the path of an aircraft, the direction of which (path) at any point is usually expressed in degrees from North (true, magnetic or grid) is known as: from question bank
explanation: Definition of track.

question_text: A first solo flight shall only be authorized when: from question bank

options.B: When it doesn't conflict with any other flight.
options.C: The Instructor present in the control tower during the flight.
options.D: All of the above are correct.

explanation: All conditions must be met.

question_text: The stop way is to be constructed to flexible pavements to a bearing strength which is equal to: from question bank
explanation: Stopway strength is 1/3 of runway.

question_text: The basic designator of an ATS Route shall consist of one letter of the letter from the alphabet followed by a number from 1 to 999. Which of the following alphabets indicate that the route is an area navigation route which form the part of regional network: from question bank
explanation: Area navigation routes are designated with letters L, M, N, P.

question_text: A method of navigation which permits aircraft operation on any desired flight path with in the coverage of station -- referenced navigation aids or within the limits of the capability of self-contained aids, or a combination of these is known as: from question bank
explanation: Definition of Area Navigation (RNAV).

question_text: The basic designator of an ATS Route shall consist of one letter of the letter from the alphabet followed by a number from 1 to 999. Which of the following alphabets indicate that the route does form the part of regional network but is not an area navigation route? from question bank
explanation: Routes with letters A, B, G, R are non-RNAV regional routes.

question_text: A side clearance of feet on either side of the runway is to be maintained free of all obstructions for the full length of the runway including over-run. from question bank
explanation: Side clearance is 750 ft.

question_text: The outer margin of the side clearance 750 ft feet on either side of the runway is known as: from question bank
explanation: The outer margin is the runway clearance line.

question_text: A defined area where aircraft can be held, or bypassed, to facilitate efficient surface movement of aircraft is known as: from question bank
explanation: Holding bay is defined.

question_text: One Meter is equal to feet. from question bank
explanation: 1 m = 3.28084 ft, approximately 3.281.

question_text: One Nautical Mile is equal to: from question bank
explanation: 1 NM = 1.15 statute miles.

question_text: One Kilogram is equal to: from question bank
One Kilometer is equal: from question bank

options.D: Both b) and c) are correct.

explanation: 1 km = 0.54 NM and 0.62 statute mile.

question_text: An airspace of defined dimensions, above land areas or territorial waters of a State, within which the flight of aircraft is restricted in accordance with certain specified conditions, is know as: from question bank
explanation: Restricted area has specified conditions.

question_text: Who shall give way to other aircraft when two aircraft are converging at approximately same level. from question bank

options.A: Aircraft that has other aircraft on its left.
options.B: Aircraft that has other aircraft on its right.
options.C: Aircraft that has other aircraft on its left and right.
options.D: Aircraft that has other aircraft on its 9 O' clock.

explanation: Aircraft with other on right gives way (right-of-way rule).

question_text: At Lahore we have LEMOM 3 A departure for Runway 36 R. The validity indicator Three (3) indicates that: from question bank

options.A: A change has been made from the previous 2nd version.
options.B: There are three types of LEMOM departures.
options.C: The aircraft cannot proceed on LEMOM 1 or 2 Departure due traffic.
options.D: It is the original Designator of the LEMOM departure.

explanation: The number indicates version (3rd version).

question_text: A reporting plan which establishes the conditions of ADS-C data reporting (i.e. data required by the air traffic services unit and frequency of ADS-C reports which have to be agreed to prior to using ADS-C in the provision of air traffic services) is known as: from question bank
explanation: Definition of ADS-C agreement.

question_text: A means by which the terms of an ADS-C agreement will be exchanged between the ground system and the aircraft, via a data link, specifying under what conditions ADS-C reports would be initiated, and what data would be contained in the reports is known as: from question bank
explanation: That is an ADS contract.

question_text: When it is requested or necessary to for a helicopter to proceed at a slow speed above the surface air taxiing maybe authorized, normally: from question bank
explanation: Air-taxiing is normally below 20 kt and in ground effect.

question_text: An airspace of defined dimensions established around an aerodrome for the protection of aerodrome traffic is known as: from question bank
explanation: Definition of Aerodrome Traffic Zone.

question_text: An aircraft system based on secondary surveillance radar (SSR) transponder signals which operates independently of ground-based equipment to provide advice to the pilot on potential conflicting aircraft that are equipped with SSR transponders is known as: from question bank
explanation: ACAS (Airborne Collision Avoidance System) is defined.

question_text: Except where special VFR clearance is issued, VFR flights shall not take-off or land at an aerodrome within a control zone or enter the aerodrome traffic zone or traffic pattern when the ground visibility is less than 05 Km and the ceiling is less than: from question bank
explanation: Ceiling must be at least 1500 ft.

question_text: The projection on the earth's surface of the path of an aircraft, the direction of which path at any point is usually expressed in degrees from North (true, magnetic or grid) is known as: from question bank
explanation: Definition of track.

question_text: Regional QNH is the actual QNH value within an altimeter region. from question bank
explanation: Regional QNH is the lowest actual QNH in the region.

question_text: The airspace between the transition altitude and the transition level is known as: from question bank
explanation: Definition of transition layer.

question_text: At any time the thickness of transition layer shall not be less than: from question bank
explanation: Minimum thickness is 1000 ft.

question_text: A pilot of an aircraft at 3000 ft above mean sea level outside aerodrome traffic zone shall set his altimeter to: from question bank
explanation: At 3000 ft AMSL, if above transition altitude, use QNE (standard pressure).

question_text: Aerodrome elevation is the elevation of the highest point at the aerodrome. from question bank
explanation: Aerodrome elevation is the elevation of the highest point of the landing area, not the entire aerodrome.

question_text: At an aerodrome QNH is 1003 Hpa and over the airfield an aircraft is maintaining F70. What will be the altitude of the aircraft? from question bank
explanation: If QNH is lower than 1013, altitude is lower than flight level. Flight level 70 = 7000 ft at standard. Difference in pressure: 1013-1003=10 hPa, approx 300 ft. So altitude = 7000 - 300 = 6700 ft.

question_text: For visual maneuvering over an aerodrome, the height is based on: from question bank

options.C: Elevation of the highest point on the landing area.
options.D: Both b) and c) are correct.

explanation: Height is referenced to aerodrome elevation or threshold elevation? Actually for visual maneuvering, height is above aerodrome elevation.

question_text: Runway of Gilgit Airport is: from question bank
explanation: Gilgit has non-instrument runway.

question_text: What is the next VFR level after F285 in RVSM airspace? from question bank

options.D: VFR operation is not permitted in RVSM airspace.

explanation: VFR flights are not permitted in RVSM airspace.

question_text: Which of the following is correct for Lahore Runway 36R where the RWY length is 3360m, Stopway is 122m and Clearway is 305m. from question bank
explanation: TORA = runway length = 3360; ASDA = TORA + stopway = 3482; TODA = TORA + clearway = 3665. So only C is correct.

question_text: The airspace classification for Hyderabad aerodrome traffic zone is: from question bank
explanation: Hyderabad ATZ is Class C.

question_text: All the VFR flights shall comply with air traffic control clearance when: from question bank

options.A: Operated in class B, C and D airspace.
options.B: Forming a part of aerodrome traffic at controlled aerodrome.
options.D: All of the above are correct.

explanation: In all those situations VFR must comply with ATC clearance.

question_text: CNS/ATM is an abbreviation of: from question bank

options.A: Communication Navigation Satellite / Air Traffic Management.
options.B: Communication Navigation System / Air Traffic Management.
options.C: Communication Navigation Surveillance / Air Traffic Management system
options.D: Communication Navigation Surveillance / Air Traffic Management.

explanation: CNS/ATM stands for Communication, Navigation, Surveillance / Air Traffic Management.

question_text: All the piston engine aircraft with maximum certified take-off mass of more than 5700 kg are to carry Ground Proximity Warning System (GPWS). from question bank
explanation: GPWS is required for turbine engine aircraft, not piston, above certain mass.

question_text: All the ATS routes are class A: from question bank
explanation: ATS routes are Class A above F155 outside control areas.

question_text: In the event of delay of in excess of the proposed departure time or EET to be spent on ground at intermediate stop, a new FPL should be submitted and the old be cancelled. from question bank
explanation: If delay exceeds one hour, new FPL required.

question_text: No H/C shall operate under VFR in flight visibility of less than: from question bank
explanation: Helicopter VFR visibility minimum is 1.5 km.

question_text: All the aircraft are required to establish two way communication with the concerned ACC at least prior to entry in Karachi or Lahore FIR. from question bank
explanation: 15 minutes prior to entry.

question_text: The movement of pedestrians or vehicles on the maneuvering area will be subject to authorization from: from question bank
explanation: Aerodrome controller authorizes movement on maneuvering area.

explanation: Nawabshah control zone is Class C.

question_text: A defined rectangular area on the ground at the end of take-off run available prepared as a suitable area in which an aircraft can be stopped in the case of an abandoned take-off is known as: from question bank
explanation: Definition of stopway.

question_text: Isolation bay shall not be less than from the parking, buildings or public areas. from question bank
explanation: Isolation bay distance is 100 m.

question_text: R.E.S.A is an abbreviation of: from question bank
explanation: RESA stands for Runway End Safety Area.

question_text: A.C.L is an abbreviation of: from question bank
explanation: ACL stands for Altimeter Check Location.

question_text: Which of the following requirement is correct for fire category 9? from question bank
explanation: All quantities are correct for fire category 9.

question_text: Fire prevention vehicle Barracuda can carry: from question bank
explanation: Barracuda carries all those.

question_text: Selection of the Runway-in-Use is the responsibility of: from question bank
explanation: Aerodrome controller selects runway-in-use.

question_text: Normally the aircraft shall land and take-off into the wind but the aerodrome controller can select runway-in-use on which aircraft do not land and take-off into the wind depending on: from question bank

options.D: All of the above are correct.

explanation: All factors may influence runway selection.

question_text: If the QNH value falls lower than the standard pressure value i.e. 1013.2 Hpa than the thickness of the transition layer will: from question bank
explanation: Lower QNH reduces transition layer thickness.

question_text: Required discharge rate for Fire category 9 is: from question bank
explanation: Fire Cat 9 requires 4500 L/min.

question_text: Response time for RFF vehicles to reach the site is: from question bank

options.A: Not more the 2 min and 30 seconds.
options.C: Not more than 3 minutes and 30 seconds.

explanation: Response time is ≤3 minutes.

question_text: The actual path of the aeroplane flown over the ground is known as: from question bank
explanation: Track made good is the actual path over ground.

question_text: Which of the following is a lift augmentation device? from question bank
explanation: All are lift augmentation devices.

question_text: Function of spoiler at the leading edge is to spoil the lift producing capability of the wing. from question bank
explanation: Spoilers reduce lift by disrupting airflow.

question_text: Spoilers are used to reduce the speed of the aircraft in the air. from question bank
explanation: Spoilers increase drag and reduce lift; they can reduce speed but are primarily for lift dump and roll control.

question_text: Spoilers are used after landing to enable the aircraft to stop in a comparatively short distance. from question bank
explanation: Spoilers increase drag and reduce lift, aiding braking.

question_text: Aircraft which have double wings are called biplanes. from question bank
explanation: Biplanes have two wings.

question_text: A flight which is subject to an ATC Clearance is called a: from question bank
explanation: Definition of controlled flight.

options.C: Airspace Classes A, B and C

explanation: Own separation is allowed in Class D and E.

question_text: The lateral limit of CTR shall extend to at least: from question bank

options.A: 10 NM from the centre of the aerodrome in the direction which approaches may be made.
options.B: 5 NM from the centre of the aerodrome or aerodromes in the direction from which the approaches may be made.
options.C: 15 NM from the centre of the aerodrome.
options.D: 25 NM from the centre of the aerodrome.

explanation: CTR lateral limit is at least 5 NM in approach direction.

question_text: If a control zone is located within lateral limits of a control area it shall extend upwards from: from question bank

options.B: 1000 feet above the highest obstruction in control zone.
options.C: Surface of the earth ( ground or water )
options.D: Any level determined by the appropriate authority.

explanation: Control zone extends from surface.

question_text: The lower limit of control area shall be established at a height above ground or water of not less then: from question bank
explanation: Lower limit of control area is 700 ft AGL.

options.A: All aircraft provided with Air Traffic Control Service.
options.B: In so far as practicable to all other aircraft having filed a flight plan or otherwise known to the air traffic services.
options.C: To any aircraft known or believed to be the subject of unlawful interference.

explanation: Alerting service provided to all those.

explanation: VFR levels: for tracks 180-359, odd levels +500? Actually F65 is for westbound tracks (180-359).

explanation: VOR frequency range is 108-118 MHz.

explanation: Entry is according to heading, not bearing.

options.A: From end of November to end of March.
options.B: From end of October to end of January.

options.B: Personnel crossing close to runway, which constitute hazard to the concerned aircraft.
options.C: Any other aircraft constituting hazard to the concerned aircraft.

options.D: During day and night irrespective of visibility and weather conditions.

options.A: It is in class A airspace.
options.C: It forms a part of regional network.

question_text: Unless otherwise instructed all turns in the standard race track holding pattern shall be made: from question bank

options.C: In accordance with the entry pattern.

explanation: EET is estimated elapsed time.

explanation: That is Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA).

question_text: The time at which it is estimated that a VFR flight will arrive over the aerodrome is called its: from question bank
explanation: VFR ETA is over the aerodrome.

question_text: AIP stands for: from question bank

question_text: AIM stands for: from question bank
explanation: AIM stands for Aeronautical Information Management.

question_text: AIC stands for: from question bank
explanation: AIC stands for Aeronautical Information Circular.

question_text: PCN stands for: from question bank

explanation: Definition of distress phase.

question_text: Distress alerts may be received by the RCC from various sources: from question bank

options.A: Detection by the COSPAS-SARSAT satellite system;
options.C: Direct communications from the public or the distressed aircraft; or

explanation: All sources.

question_text: When a distress phase has been transferred by ATS, the RCC should: from question bank

options.A: Initiate or continue any appropriate actions normally performed during the Uncertainty and Alert Phases;
options.B: Ensure an SMC has been appointed and that all interested parties have been informed of the incident;
options.C: Examine the detailed plans of operation for the conduct of SAR operations;
options.D: Determine the availability of SAR assets to conduct SAR operations and attempt to obtain more assets if a need for them is anticipated;

explanation: All actions.

question_text: Where uncertainty exists, evaluation is usually more difficult and time consuming because of the many factors involved. from question bank
explanation: All factors.

question_text: Search planning involves the following steps: from question bank

options.A: Evaluating the situation, including the results of any previous searching.
options.B: Estimating the distress incident location and probable error of that location.
options.C: Estimating the survivors' post-distress movements and probable error of that estimate.

explanation: All steps.

question_text: A search typically involves various stages: from question bank

options.B: Stage-2 Nominated area either side of track.

explanation: All stages.

question_text: The stage one or initial search normally consists of (IMMEDIATE RESPONSE): from question bank

options.A: Action to detect by visual search or a signal from an emergency beacon;
options.C: Coordination with Police, ATS Units and other agencies as appropriate.

explanation: All are part.

question_text: Once the search area has been determined, a systematic search for the target should be planned as per following factors: from question bank

options.A: Sweep width and Type of target
options.B: Met visibility and Type of terrain
options.C: Search aircraft speed and Fatigue factor
options.D: Cloud cover and Position of the sun

explanation: All factors.

question_text: The primary purpose of any SAR action is: from question bank

options.A: To reduce delay in landing/takeoff traffic.
options.B: The speedy return to a place for safety of the survivors of a distress situation.
options.C: To include medically qualified persons in the rescue team.
options.D: To provide SAR trainings to relevant Authorities.

explanation: Primary purpose is to return survivors to safety.

question_text: The SAR Mission Coordinator (SMC) shall not ensure that proper attention is given to the preparation and execution of the rescue effort. from question bank
explanation: SMC must ensure proper attention.

question_text: When it is known that an aircraft has crashed... from question bank

options.A: RCC shall confirm the crash site and ensure the provision of all assistance
options.B: Next of kin will not be kept informed
options.C: RCC shall not endeavor to arrange security at the crash site
options.D: Police are not responsible for securing the accident scene.

explanation: RCC must confirm site and provide assistance.

question_text: After proper and complete planning land party should.... from question bank

options.A: Be taken to a locality as near as possible to the distress scene
options.B: The police will determine equipment necessary for land rescue parties.
options.C: Portable radio capable of communicating with other SAR Units should always be included in a rescue party's equipment.
options.D: In cases where all occupants of a crashed aircraft are not immediately accounted for, search for missing persons must be continued.

explanation: All are correct.

question_text: The aircraft wreckage should be disturbed to assist in the recovery of survivors. from question bank
explanation: Wreckage should not be disturbed unnecessarily.

question_text: Pakistan Maritime Security Agency (PMSA) is responsible for the coordination of surface vessels engaged in the rescue of survivors in or on the sea: from question bank
explanation: PMSA coordinates surface vessels.

question_text: When both maritime rescue units and helicopters are dispatched to the same distress scene, it is not advisable to transfer survivors to the helicopters for a more rapid delivery to medical facilities. from question bank
explanation: It is advisable to transfer for rapid delivery.

question_text: When considering use of aircraft for recovery of survivors: from question bank

options.A: The rescue aircraft and crew should not be exposed to inordinate danger.
options.B: Fixed wing aircraft should only be used when there is a suitable aerodrome or landing area near the scene.
options.C: Pilots shall attempt to land at other than prepared landing areas
options.D: Specialist advice concerning the operation shall not be obtained.

explanation: None are correct; pilots should not attempt unprepared areas without advice.

question_text: SAR Operations conclude when: from question bank

options.A: The target is located and the survivors are rescued;
options.B: If there was no distress, the beacon has been turned off;
options.C: Information is received that the target is no longer in distress;
options.D: All known persons on board are accounted for,

explanation: All are termination conditions.

question_text: The authority to end a search rests with different levels within the SAR organization: from question bank
explanation: Correct.

question_text: When the target of a search action has been located and the survivors removed to a place of safety, the RCC shall ensure: from question bank

options.B: Next of kin are fully informed;
options.C: Arrangements made for the recovery of dropped survival equipment

explanation: All actions.

question_text: When it is determined that further search would be of no avail, the SMC shall consider recommending the suspension or termination of the SAR operation. from question bank
explanation: Correct.

question_text: When a SAR action is discontinued or a search is suspended, the RCC shall not inform all authorities, units and facilities that have been activated and/or alerted. from question bank
explanation: Must inform all.

question_text: Reopening of a suspended case should be considered if significant new information is received. from question bank
explanation: Correct.

question_text: Post incident debriefs should be used to establish opportunities for improvement in the operation of the National SAR System: from question bank
explanation: Correct.

question_text: The purpose of training for all personnel employed on SAR missions is to meet SAR system objectives by developing SAR specialists. from question bank
explanation: Correct.

question_text: Formal training of SAR personnel should: from question bank

options.A: focus on both the practical and theoretical application of SAR
options.B: focus on the practical application of SAR
options.C: focus on theoretical application of SAR

explanation: Both practical and theoretical.

question_text: SAR training exercises provide: from question bank

options.A: learning experience and improve liaison and coordination skills.
options.B: help demonstrate and assess the competence of the SAR service.
options.C: reveal deficiencies that may exist in SAR plans

explanation: All benefits.

question_text: Regular conduct of joint SAR exercises between SAR Organizations should form a part of any training program. from question bank
explanation: Correct.

question_text: Contracting States shall delineate Search and Rescue Regions (SRRs) and established Rescue Coordination Centers (RCCs) and Rescue Sub Centers (RSCs) for the provisioning of SAR services within SRRs. from question bank
explanation: Correct.

question_text: States may consider combining their SAR resources into a Join RCC (JRCC), responsible for both Aeronautical and Maritime SAR incidents. from question bank
explanation: Correct.

question_text: RCC may be co-located close to a well equipped center such as Flight Information Center (FIC) or Area Control Center (ACC), so that additional communication facilities can be kept to a minimum. from question bank
explanation: Correct.

question_text: ATC Team Leader may not be assigned the responsibility to supervise RCC. from question bank
explanation: ATC Team Leader may supervise RCC.

question_text: The RCC shall be manned by each ATC Team for maintaining 24 / 7 SAR Services. from question bank
explanation: Correct.
